# 6 Most Common Errors in Implementing Fire Safety Plans

by Benjamin Ilvonen

Fire safety engineering errors are very rarely a matter of design error. Most often they arise from information gaps between the work of different designers, procurement, and installers or from conflicting but overlapping designs. Moreover, the risk of errors in the implementation of fire safety plans escalates as the number of designers and contractors increases in large construction projects.

**Here are the six most common errors in implementing fire safety plans:**

**Prevent Errors with On-Site Audits**  
  
Careful fire engineering alone will not eliminate fire safety defects in your building. As we have illustrated, many errors in fire safety design are actually errors in implementation of the fire technical plan on site. On-site audits of installations and design compliance play a significant role in preventing errors, clarifying the overall picture and providing reassurance that the building will perform as it should in the event of an accident.   
  
Jensen Hughes works with building owners, developers and architects to undertake detailed fire safety audits that meet latest regulations. Our team is involved early in the design process to identify potential compliance issues and develop effective and efficient fire safety solutions that meet design needs. Learn more about our [fire safety audit and risk assessment services.](https://www.jensenhughes.com/europe/services/fire-risk-assessments)

# Barriers and Facilitators for Implementation of Individualized Fire Safety (IFS) in Sweden

### Affiliations

# Barriers and Facilitators for Implementation of Individualized Fire Safety (IFS) in Sweden

### Authors

### Affiliations

## Abstract

In 2010, the Swedish Civil Contingencies Agency (MSB) announced a "vision zero" of zero fire deaths in Sweden by 2050. Studies into fire deaths have identified that certain risk groups, including but not limited to older people, are overrepresented in fire death statistics in Sweden. The MSB has developed guidelines for how individualised fire safety (IFS) can be implemented in local communities for risk groups, in support of their vision zero for fire deaths. This paper presents the results of an interview study with a selection of Swedish municipalities to further explore how municipalities are working with IFS programs for community dwelling older people. The Consolidated Framework for Implementation Research has been used to analyse data developed through semi-structured interviews, from an analysis of the delegation of authority from MSB to local level and assessment of secondary documentation from national, regional and local organisations. The analysis has identified that IFS has, indeed, been implemented to varying degrees in Sweden, but that there are both facilitators and barriers which can be further leveraged to improve the implementation of IFS in the future.

**Keywords:**
CFIR; Consolidated Framework for Implementation Research; Implementation research; Personalized fire safety; Vision zero.

© The Author(s) 2021.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Common barriers include lack of established networks, insufficient communication between stakeholders, and inadequate training, hindering effective fire safety plan execution.
Current fire safety technologies might not integrate seamlessly, leading to gaps in safety measures and challenges in adapting to diverse building designs and materials.
Ongoing research is crucial to develop innovative fire safety solutions, address emerging hazards, and enhance existing frameworks for better risk management and prevention strategies.
